coredata.dc:title: Self-photo-oxidation for extending visible light absorption of carbon dots and oxidase-like activity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jinyi
Lin, Yao
Wu, Shihong
Hou, Xiandeng
Zheng, Chengbin
Wu, Peng
Liu, Juewen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) are attractive for their low cost, excellent optical properties, and low toxicity, but most C-dots are limited by their UV absorbance, especially for photodynamic therapy (PDT) applications. Developing rational and simple strategies to obtain C-dots with longer excitation/emission wavelengths remains a challenge. Previous work showed that C-dots can produce a high yield of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon UV light illumination. We herein show that the produced ROS can lead to further oxidation and growth of C-dots, leading to visible light absorption (400 nm–650 nm) and extend fluorescence emission from blue to green. In addition, the light-treated C-dots showed boosted peroxidase and oxidase mimicking activity than the initial C-dots in the absence of light. In the presence of visible light, the catalytic efficiency of the light-treated C-dots is 11-fold much higher than the pristine C-dots. The light-treated C-dots can be used as a PDT agent due to the extended excitation and visible photo-oxidative activity, simultaneously providing a highly efficient cancer therapy. This study provides a simple way to modify C-dots leading to novel properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Visible light absorption
Self-photo-oxidation
Nanozyme
Catalytical activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of blue and green emitting C-dots
UV light-treated C-dots showing visible light absorption and boosted light-driven nanozyme activities
Structural and emissive changes of the C-dots upon UV illumination
Boosted nanozyme activities (no light during reactions)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis spectra obtained as a function of time on 365 nm illumination of C-dots solution (50 μg/mL, inset: 300 μg/mL). Photographs of TMB oxidation by the pristine C-dots and light-treated C-dots (5 μg/mL) with light (365 nm, 400 nm, 420 nm, 450 nm, 470 nm, 500 nm, 530 nm, and white light) at pH 4 for different times (20–120 s). Bottom panel: the absorbance ratio of these samples. A1 and A2 are the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm produced by the pristine C-dots and light-treated C-dots, respectively. The white light is produced by a composite LED. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A, B) TEM micrographs, (C, D) 3D fluorescence spectra, (E) XPS spectra, and (F) FT-IR spectra of the pristine C-dots and the light-treated C-dots. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The kinetics of oxidation of TMB (0.8 mM) by the pristine C-dots and light-treated C-dots (80 μg/mL) without or with H2O2 (1 mM) at pH 4 (acetic buffer, 20 mM). Insets are the photographs of the four samples after 4 h of reaction. (B) The absorbance of oxidation of TMB (0.8 mM) by different wavelength LED light-treated C-dots (80 μg/mL) with H2O2 (1 mM) at pH 4 (acetic buffer, 20 mM). (C) The kinetics of oxidation of TMB by 365 nm light-treated C-dots (different processing time: 0–300 s) with H2O2 at pH 4, and the rate is 0.0079 s−1. (D) The absorbance of oxidation of TMB by 365 nm light-treated C-dots processed at different pH. pH 2, 4, 7: acetate buffer 20 mM, pH 10: Tris-HCl buffer 20 mM, time: 300 s). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Dose-dependent PDT effects on the viability of A375 cells: (A) pristine C-dots, and (B) light-treated C-dots in the concentration range 5–100 μg/mL. Cell apoptosis analysis of (C) cells in the dark, (D) cells after illumination with 500 nm LED, (E) pristine C-dots, and (F) light-treated C-dots mediated PDT by Annexin V-FITC/PI staining and subsequent flow cytometry assay. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic illustration of (A) self-photo-oxidation of C-dots, which converts the blue-emitting C-dots to green emitting ones. (B) Various nanozyme activities of the green-emitting C-dots. (C) PDT using visible light for killing cancer cells.
A comparison of the steady-state enzyme kinetic parameters of pristine C-dots and light-treated C-dots (L-C-dots).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: [E] is the concentration of C-dots. Km is the Michaelis constant. Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity. kcat is the catalytic constant. kcat = Vmax/[E]. kcat ratio means kcat (L-C-dots)/kcat (C-dots).

head.ce:title: Self-photo-oxidation for extending visible light absorption of carbon dots and oxidase-like activity

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jinyi Zhang and Yao Lin contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) are attractive for their low cost, excellent optical properties, and low toxicity, but most C-dots are limited by their UV absorbance, especially for photodynamic therapy (PDT) applications. Developing rational and simple strategies to obtain C-dots with longer excitation/emission wavelengths remains a challenge. Previous work showed that C-dots can produce a high yield of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon UV light illumination. We herein show that the produced ROS can lead to further oxidation and growth of C-dots, leading to visible light absorption (400 nm–650 nm) and extend fluorescence emission from blue to green. In addition, the light-treated C-dots showed boosted peroxidase and oxidase mimicking activity than the initial C-dots in the absence of light. In the presence of visible light, the catalytic efficiency of the light-treated C-dots is 11-fold much higher than the pristine C-dots. The light-treated C-dots can be used as a PDT agent due to the extended excitation and visible photo-oxidative activity, simultaneously providing a highly efficient cancer therapy. This study provides a simple way to modify C-dots leading to novel properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) have drawn increasing attention over the last decade owing to their excellent optical properties, water-solubility, and biocompatibility [1–4], allowing a wide variety of applications including sensing, therapeutics, optoelectronics, and photocatalysis [5–9]. Most water-soluble C-dots absorb in the UV region and emit blue light. It would be useful to obtain C-dots with longer excitation and emission wavelengths. Besides, C-dots were rarely used as an oxidizing or reducing agent [10,11]. To improve the properties of C-dots, the main strategies are to use different starting materials [12–14], and perform post-synthesis modifications. For example, poly-(ethylene glycol)-coated C-dots showed brighter photoluminescence [15]. Se or Te-doped C-dots allowed a high antioxidation activity for scavenging free radicals [16,17]. Protein, lipid or cyclodextrin-modified C-dots showed versatile applications in biocatalysis [7,18–20]. However, these methods are often expensive and sometimes complex to perform.

Nanomaterials with enzyme-mimicking properties, also known as nanozymes, have attracted considerable attention due to their higher stability and lower cost than natural protein enzymes [21–24]. Light-activated nanozymes with the advantages of light-regulated activity and using molecular oxygen as a green oxidant were used in biosensing, pollutant removal, cleavage of DNA, and cancer photodynamic therapy (PDT) [25,26]. While PDT shows important benefits of minimal invasiveness, low toxicity, and widespread applicability [27–29], the UV excitation of blue-emitting of C-dots greatly limited their application in PDT. Our previous work showed that C-dots can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon light illumination, which offered strong photo-oxidation activities [30,31]. The produced ROS may lead to further crosslinking and oxidation of C-dots, which may be a simple way to modify the C-dots leading to novel properties. While radical-induced self-assembly is a key concept in supramolecular chemistry [32,33], it's effect in C-dots has yet to be examined.

Herein, we used a 365 nm light-emitting diode (LED) to treat blue-emitting C-dots for a short time (Scheme 1 ). Surprisingly, green-emitting C-dots with extended visible light absoprtion (∼650 nm) were obtained. An absorption band in the visible region emerged after the light treatment, allowing the use of visible light for efficient photo-oxidation and PDT, simultaneously providing a highly efficient material for cancer therapy. In addition, the light-treated C-dots showed boosted peroxidase and oxidase mimicking activities in the absence of light.

Ethylenediamine, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), hydrogen peroxide (30 wt%), cerium (III) chloride, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and rose bengal were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, USA). Citric acid and sodium acetate were purchased from Mandel Scientific (Guelph, ON, Canada). Milli-Q water was used for all the experiments to prepare buffers and solutions. Annexin V-FITC/PI and CCK8 were purchased from KeyGen Biotech (Jiangsu, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue and green emitting C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The blue-emitting C-dots were synthesized via a popular method of hydrothermal polymerization of ethylenediamine and citric acid [5,30]. Ethylenediamine (168 μL) and citric acid (0.5 g) were dissolved in Milli-Q water (5 mL). The solution was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave (20 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the reactor was opened and the obtained products were further purified by column chromatography (silica 400 mesh, water as the eluent). Finally, the C-dot powders were obtained after freeze-drying. For control experiments, green-emitting C-dots were synthesized via the solvothermal polymerization of o-phenylenediamine in ethanol [12]. In brief, o-phenylenediamine (0.1 g) was dissolved in ethanol (10 mL). Then the solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, removal of solvents and further drying under vacuum, the green-emitting C-dot powders were obtained.

The above-prepared blue-emitting C-dots (300 μg/mL, 1 mL) in acetate buffer (20 mM, pH 4.0) were added to a quartz colorimetric dish and irradiated by LED lights (3V, 3W) with different emission wavelengths for different time periods. The obtained light-treated C-dots with dark brown color were diluted and directly used for catalytic experiments, or were treated with freeze-drying for further characterization.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots were recorded by a spectrometer (Agilent 8453A). Zeta-potential measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano 90 instrument (Malvern). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI Co., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with an AXIS Ultra DLD 800 X (Kratos, UK). The FT-IR spectra (KBr pellets) were collected with a Nicolet IS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Inc., America). The three dimension (3D) fluorescence spectra, blue fluorescence emission spectra (Ex: 370 nm), and green fluorescence emission spectra (Ex: 430 nm) of C-dots were collected with a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). The excitation and emission ranges of 3D fluorescence spectra were 310–422 nm and 430–610 nm, respectively. The fluorescence decay spectra of C-dots at 440 nm and 530 nm were measured on FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer with SpectralLED (350 nm and 450 nm) as the excitation sources. A total of 10000 counts were collected for all decays. The pulses of SpectralLED were made with 1 MHz.

Typically, C-dots (80 μg/mL) were dispersed in 186 μL of acetate buffer (pH 4, 20 mM), to which 4 μL TMB (40 mM) and 10 μL H2O2 (20 mM) were added to initiate the reaction. The absorbance at 652 nm for TMB was monitored by using a microplate reader.

A375 (melanoma) cells were incubated with different concentrations of C-dots at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 12 h. One cell culture plate was used for exploring the cytotoxicity of C-dots, while the other plates were illuminated using LED (λ = 500 nm, 3 V, 3W) for 10 min after washing away the C-dots and exchanging for a fresh medium. Then the cells were incubated with a CCK-8 diluent for 1 h and the optical density (OD) at 450 nm was measured. Cell apoptosis was determined by Annexin V-fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and propidium iodide (PI) staining kit. Briefly, 1 × 105 cells were incubated in a six-well plate for 12 h, 50 μg/mL C-dots or light-treated C-dots were added and incubated for 12 h. The plates were illuminated using LED (λ = 500 nm, 3 V, 3W) for 5 min after washing away the C-dots and exchanging for a fresh medium. After washing with PBS twice, the cells were re-suspended in 500 μL binding buffer (1 × ) and stained with 5 μL Annexin V-FITC and 5 μL PI for 15 min in the dark. Finally, the cells were analyzed by flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV light-treated C-dots showing visible light absorption and boosted light-driven nanozyme activities
ce:section.ce:para: Our C-dots were prepared via the classic method of hydrothermal polymerization of citric acid and ethylenediamine [5,30]. After UV light illumination of the C-dots (365 nm, 5 min), the color of the sample changed from light yellow (300 μg/mL) to dark brown (inset of Fig. 1 A). This color change prompted us to measure the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1A). The as-synthesized C-dots had a strong absorption peak at 350 nm. As the duration of this light illumination increased, this 350 nm peak dropped in intensity, while a new and broad absorption feature emerged extending to the visible region (400 nm–650 nm). As previously reported, the C-dots can act as a highly efficient photosensitizer to induce ultrafast oxidation of TMB under 365 nm UV light [30]. With increasing illumination wavelength, the oxidative activity gradually decreased even with a longer illumination time (Fig. 1B, first row). The need for UV light to activate the C-dots greatly limited its applications.

Since the light-treated C-dots possess visible light absorption, we further tested their photosensitive oxidation activity. For the UV-treated C-dots, all the LED lights from 365 nm to 530 nm induced the oxidative reaction (Fig. 1B, second row). The quantified absorbance ratio of oxTMB suggested that the photocatalytic activity of the UV-treated C-dots in the UV region was not significantly affected, while the activity in the visible region was increased by more than 10 times than the pristine C-dots (Fig. 1B). Therefore, these UV-treated C-dots possess boosted visible light-driven catalytic activities.

Previously, Ce3+ was reported to enhance the singlet oxygen yield of the C-dots so that the light-driven C-dots/Ce3+ system could oxide TMB at natural pH [31]. Thus, we further tested the photocatalytic activity of light-treated C-dots/Ce3+ at natural pH, and the result was consistent with the activity at acidic pH (Fig. S1). These results suggested that the visible photocatalytic activity of the light-treated C-dots was resulted from the same photochemical processes of the pristine C-dots. To verify the generality of this method, we synthesized the green-emitting C-dots (G-C-dots) using different precursors for light treatment (Fig. S2A) [12]. Similarly, the light treatment increased the absorption of the G-C-dots in all wavelength regions, and the color of the sample also got darker (Fig. S2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and emissive changes of the C-dots upon UV illumination
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanism of shifted light absorption, we then characterized the size, fluorescent emission, functional groups, and elemental composition of the C-dots before and after light treatment. In the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, most of the original C-dots were below 2 nm, while the size increased to above 3 nm after light illumination (Fig. 2 A and B). The size distribution histograms of C-dots are shown in Fig. S3. Since our C-dots were purified and the precursors were removed before light illumination, the increased size was likely due to coupling of different C-dots. The initial C-dots could be oxidized by the generated ROS upon UV light illumination to produce larger conjugated C-dots.

We reason that if such reactions happened, a mixture of various C-dots might possess multiple emission peaks, depending on the excitation wavelength. To quantitatively characterize this conversion, the 3D fluorescence spectra were measured. Most previously reported C-dots showed excitation-dependent emission, suggesting a mixture of various C-dots. Since our blue-emitting C-dots were obtained after column purification, their fluorescence emission spectra did not change with the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2C) [34]. Interestingly, two emission centers appeared in the light-treated C-dots (Fig. 2D), and the optimal excitation wavelength for the blue emission peak was significantly changed, consistent with the growth of a new emitting species. The as-synthesized C-dots had a strong blue fluorescence emission upon excitation at 370 nm, whereas this emission almost fully disappeared after the treatment by 365 nm UV light (Fig. S4A). In addition, the fluorescence lifetime at 440 nm also decreased correspondingly (Fig. S4C). Interestingly, when excited at 430 nm, the UV light treated sample showed a strong green emission and a slightly increased lifetime (Figs. S4B and S4D). Noticeable, performing the UV light treatment in a N2 atmosphere had almost no effect on the fluorescence emission or lifetime (Fig. S4), suggesting that the generated ROS might be responsible for converting the blue-emitting C-dots (small conjugated size) to the green-emitting ones (larger conjugated size).

The surface groups on the C-dots would be different before and after light treatment if oxidative reactions happened. To test this, FT-IR spectroscopy analysis of the light-treated C-dots was performed, which showed that the stretching absorption of N–H decreased, while the stretching absorption of CO and bending absorption of N–H disappeared (Fig. 2E). On the contrary, the bending absorption of O–H increased, and the stretching absorption band of N–O emerged. These results revealed that some groups of the C-dots were oxidized under the UV light treatment. XPS analysis showed that after the UV light irradiation, the oxygen content increased (Fig. 2F), further supporting the oxidation mechanism. This result suggested that the increased oxygen content was correlated with the disappearance of the blue emission (Fig. S4A). Finally, the zeta-potential of the light-treated C-dots became more negative indicating more oxygen-containing groups (Fig. S5). Based on these results, we reason that the dissolved oxygen was converted to ROS by the light-induced C-dots, and the ROS in turn oxidized the C-dots and drove their size increase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Boosted nanozyme activities (no light during reactions)
ce:section.ce:para: As a biocompatible and low toxicity nanomaterial, C-dots have been extensively studied for nanozyme activities, especially photosensitive oxidation. It was reported that C-dots have peroxidase-like activities at high temperature (H2O2 needed, 35 °C–60 °C) but low activity at room temperature [35–37]. Thus, some metal ions or molecules were used to dope or modify C-dots for higher catalytic activities [38–40]. In addition, C-dots are not known to have oxidase-mimicking activities without light. Since our light-treated C-dots were oxidized, they might have enhanced oxidation activity, and this hypothesis was then explored.

We first studied the peroxidase-like activity (with H2O2) using TMB as a substrate. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the absorbance at 652 nm of oxTMB gradually increased with the light-treated C-dots (red line), and the solution changed to blue. These results indicated that TMB was oxidized and formed a blue colored charge-transfer complex (oxTMB) [41]. In contrast, the absorbance and color change were muh smaller for the pristine C-dots (black line). In addition, the blue color started to fade after 1 h, suggesting some reducing activity of the pristine C-dots [31].

Without adding H2O2, we then studied the oxidase-like activity of the C-dots. TMB can also be oxidized by the light-treated C-dots (Fig. 3A, green line). For the pristine C-dots, however, no oxidation occurred (blue line). These experiments demonstrated that the light-treated C-dots were boosted for both the peroxidase- and oxidase-mimicking activities.

We then performed steady-state kinetic assays at different substrate concentrations to extract peroxidase-like enzyme parameters of C-dots based on the initial reaction rates. Since the catalytic efficiency of the light-treated C-dots is much higher, we used 10-fold more of the pristine C-dots to obtain a measurable rate. Typical Michaelis-Menten curves were obtained, and we fitted the data only in the first 300 s for the initial phase (Figs. S6–S7). From these data, we extracted the Michaelis constant (K m) and the maximum initial reaction rate (V max) (Table 1 ). Typically, a lower K m value indicates a higher affinity between the enzyme and the substrate. After UV light treating, the C-dots showed a lower affinity towards TMB, but a higher affinity for H2O2. Considering the pristine C-dots had a high concentration, the catalytic constant k cat value of the light-treated C-dots was ∼11-fold higher compared to C-dots for TMB, and ∼7-fold higher for H2O2.

The above experiments were performed using the C-dots illuminated with a 365 nm LED for 5 min. Here, we used the peroxidase-like activity of the C-dots to optimize the wavelength, time of illumination and pH. First, different LED lights up to 570 nm were used to treat the pristine C-dots for 5 min, and then TMB and H2O2 were added to these samples. These obtained light-treated C-dots exhibited higher peroxidase-like activity than the pristine C-dots (Fig. 3B). Noticeable, with the increase of wavelength of LED lights, the catalytic activity of the light-treated C-dots decreased. Therefore, the UV LED induced the best peroxidase-like activity. This result was in good agreement with the conversion mechanism by ROS, which was directly related to light absorption. Then, the UV LED was chosen for treating the C-dots for different time periods. In just 5 s of illumination, the absorbance of oxTMB by treated the C-dots was twice of that of the pristine C-dots (Fig. 3C). With the increase of illumination time, the activity increased and saturated at around 5 min. Similarly, the blue and green fluorescence emission of the C-dots changed rapidly and the change rate decreased gradually with the increase of illumination time (Fig. S8).

C-dots are very sensitive to pH due to the ionization of surface carboxylic acid and amino groups. Different pH may change the surface properties of C-dots and thus affect the light-induced internal conversion reaction. The light-induced internal conversion reaction may be related to pH as well. Therefore, we explored the effect of pH (2, 4, 7, and 10). After UV light treatment, the fluorescence spectra of the C-dots and light-treated C-dots at different pH were measured (Figs. S9–S12). At pH 7, UV-induced blue emission quenching was the highest, while at pH 4, green emission enhancement reached the highest. The peroxidase-like activity of these UV light-treated C-dots at different pH were further explored. The light-treated C-dots at pH 4 showed the highest activity for the oxidation of TMB with H2O2 (Fig. 3D), which was consistent with the result of enhanced green emission.

A potential application of C-dots is for PDT, although it was limited by the need of UV light. Polythiophene- [42–44] or porphyrin-based [45] C-dots have high singlet oxygen yields and provide highly efficient visible cancer therapy, but preparing such special C-dots involved complex chemical synthesis. Although our C-dots generated more ROS and can be further enhanced by the addition of mediators [31,46], the need of UV excitation greatly limits their application in vivo. Upconversion photoluminescence of C-dots was also studied [12,47], but low near-infrared absorption and expensive equipment restrict its applications.

Our UV light-treated C-dots possessed better optical stability compared to pristine C-dots and rose bengal (Fig. S13). In addition, the increased photocatalytic activity in the visible region makes the light-treated C-dots an excellent candidate for PDT. Herein, the skin cancer cell line A375 was employed to demonstrate the PDT efficiency of C-dots. The cytotoxicity and PDT efficiency of the pristine C-dots and light-treated C-dots against A375 cells were evaluated by CCK-8. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A and B, both types of C-dots had little adverse effects on the survival of A375 cells in the dark even at a very high concentration of 100 μg/mL. After LED light (500 nm, 3 V, 3 W) illumination for 15 min, about 30% cell death was observed with 100 μg/mL pristine C-dots mediated PDT. In contrast, only 20 μg/mL the light-treated C-dots induced about 40% cell death. A killing rate of 85% was obtained when the light-treated C-dots were increased to 100 μg/mL.

It is believed that the generated ROS by light-driven C-dots played a vital role in the cancer cell killing process [48]. To further confirm the pathway of cell death, an Annexin V-FITC and PI staining kit was employed [48]. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, neither the Annexin V-FITC nor PI-stained cells were detected in control assays. After C-dot and light-treated C-dot mediated PDT, the percentage of Annexin V positive cells (Q3 areas) was obviously increased, indicating that apoptosis was the major way of cell death (Fig. 4E and F). Moreover, the higher apoptosis rate by the light-treated C-dots indicated a higher visible photo-oxidative activity than the pristine C-dots. Therefore, the data suggested that the generated ROS led to cell apoptosis.

In summary, we found that the generated ROS by light-illumination of C-dots can induce self-oxidation and a size increase of the C-dots. The obtained larger conjugated C-dots showed a broad visible region absorption and green fluorescence emission. Interestingly, the light treatment boosted the peroxidase and oxidase mimicking activities of the C-dots. Due to the increased visible photo-oxidative activity, the light-treated C-dots possessed a high PDT efficiency upon visible light excitation. Both types of C-dots havd little effects on the survival of cells in the dark indicating high biocompatibility and low toxicity. These simple light-modified C-dots possessed several novel properties, extending the application of C-dots. The light-induced self-oxidation articulated the self-modification properties of C-dots, which will shine light on other related research in the future. For example, adding appropriate molecules or other nanomaterials in the light-induced self-oxidation process of C-dots might help obtain hybrid nanomaterials with designer properties.

Jinyi Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yao Lin: Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Shihong Wu: Investigation. Xiandeng Hou: Resources. Chengbin Zheng: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Peng Wu: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Juewen Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding for this work was from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC), and the International Science and Technology Cooperation Project of Sichuan Province (No. 2021YFH0124).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.06.053.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, Z. Kang, Y. Liu, S.-T. Lee, Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications, J. Mater. Chem. 22 (2012) 24230-24253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
